description_long: This object is a sculpted guard stone, likely made from a single block of weathered stone, featuring a prominent lion's head and mane in high relief. The mane is intricately carved, with deep grooves and curls, while the body is more simply rendered, showing some anatomical details and a smooth, rounded back. The guard stone is attached to a rectangular slab, which serves as its base and mounting surface, suggesting it would be installed upright against a wall or at the edge of a pathway. The surface shows signs of aging, with discoloration, minor chipping, and areas of erosion, especially on the protruding features of the mane and head. The coloration is predominantly a mottled gray and white, with darker streaks and patches likely due to weather exposure and pollution. There are no visible inscriptions or decorative engravings apart from the sculpted lion motif.
description: A weathered stone guard stone featuring a sculpted lion's head and mane, mounted on a rectangular slab. The surface is mottled gray-white with signs of age and erosion.
description_view_0: Top-down view showing the rounded back and detailed mane, with the rectangular slab visible along one side.
description_view_1: Side view highlighting the lion's mane and the attachment to the rectangular slab, with anatomical details of the body visible.
description_view_2: Underside view revealing the base of the slab and the underside of the mane, with shadowed recesses and less detail.
description_view_3: End view focusing on the back of the head and mane, with the slab's thickness and attachment point visible.
attribute_location: Typically placed at the corners of urban streets, at the entrance to driveways, or adjacent to building facades in historical districts to protect structures from vehicle impact.
attribute_installation_orientation: Meant to be installed upright, partially embedded in the ground or flush with a curb or wall for stability and protection.
attribute_wear_patterns: Visible chipping and smoothing on the protruding mane and head, with discoloration and erosion likely caused by repeated vehicle contact, weather exposure, and pollution. The base and lower areas show more grime and abrasion from ground contact.
